title: Tablet computer

infobox.value: or simply **tab**, is a [mobile device](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device"), typically with a [mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system") and [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") display processing [circuitry](/wiki/Circuitry "Circuitry"), and a [rechargeable battery](/wiki/Rechargeable_battery "Rechargeable battery") in a single, thin and flat package. Tablets, being computers, have similar capabilities, but lack some [input/output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") (I/O) abilities that others have. Many modern tablets are based on [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), the only differences being that tablets are relatively larger than smartphones, with screens from 7 inches (18 cm) to 13 inches (33 cm), measured diagonally,[[1]](#cite_note-Editors_PC_Magazine-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Editors_Dictionary.com-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and may not support access to a [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"). Unlike [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") (which have traditionally run off operating systems usually designed for desktops), tablets usually run mobile operating systems, alongside smartphones.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mobile computer with integrated display, circuitry, and battery

For the computer input device, see [Graphics tablet](/wiki/Graphics_tablet "Graphics tablet"). For other uses, see [Tablet](/wiki/Tablet_(disambiguation) "Tablet (disambiguation)").

"Tablet PC" redirects here. For the class of Microsoft Windows-based tablets, see [Microsoft Tablet PC](/wiki/Microsoft_Tablet_PC "Microsoft Tablet PC").

[![Ipad Mini 6](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/IPad_Mini_6_-_1.jpg/250px-IPad_Mini_6_-_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:IPad_Mini_6_-_1.jpg)

[iPad Mini (6th generation)](/wiki/IPad_Mini_(6th_generation) "IPad Mini (6th generation)")

A **tablet computer**, commonly shortened to **tablet** or simply **tab**, is a [mobile device](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device"), typically with a [mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system") and [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") display processing [circuitry](/wiki/Circuitry "Circuitry"), and a [rechargeable battery](/wiki/Rechargeable_battery "Rechargeable battery") in a single, thin and flat package. Tablets, being computers, have similar capabilities, but lack some [input/output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") (I/O) abilities that others have. Many modern tablets are based on [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), the only differences being that tablets are relatively larger than smartphones, with screens from 7 inches (18 cm) to 13 inches (33 cm), measured diagonally,[[1]](#cite_note-Editors_PC_Magazine-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Editors_Dictionary.com-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) and may not support access to a [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"). Unlike [laptops](/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") (which have traditionally run off operating systems usually designed for desktops), tablets usually run mobile operating systems, alongside smartphones.

The touchscreen display is operated by [gestures](/wiki/Gesture_recognition "Gesture recognition") executed by finger or [digital pen](/wiki/Digital_pen "Digital pen") (stylus), instead of the [mouse](/wiki/Computer_mouse "Computer mouse"), [touchpad](/wiki/Touchpad "Touchpad"), and [keyboard](/wiki/Keyboard_(computing) "Keyboard (computing)") of larger computers. Portable computers can be classified according to the presence and appearance of physical keyboards. Two species of tablet, the *slate* and *booklet*, do not have physical keyboards and usually accept text and other input by use of a [virtual keyboard](/wiki/Virtual_keyboard "Virtual keyboard") shown on their touchscreen displays. To compensate for their lack of a physical keyboard, most tablets can connect to independent physical keyboards by [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") or [USB](/wiki/USB "USB"); [2-in-1 PCs](#2-in-1) have keyboards, distinct from tablets.

The form of the tablet was conceptualized in the middle of the 20th century ([Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") depicted fictional tablets in the 1968 science fiction film *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")*) and prototyped and developed in the last two decades of that century. In 2010, [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") released the [iPad](/wiki/IPad_(1st_generation) "IPad (1st generation)"), the first mass-market tablet to achieve widespread popularity.[[5]](#cite_note-Apple_PR_2-5) Thereafter, tablets rapidly rose in ubiquity and soon became a large product category used for personal, educational and workplace applications.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Popular uses for a tablet PC include viewing presentations, video-conferencing, reading e-books, watching movies, sharing photos, and more.[[7]](#cite_note-7) As of 2021 there are 1.28 billion tablet users worldwide according to data provided by [Statista](/wiki/Statista "Statista").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Currently Apple holds the largest manufacturer market share followed by [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") and [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of tablet computers](/wiki/History_of_tablet_computers "History of tablet computers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Telautograph-1888_electronic_writing_device.png/250px-Telautograph-1888_electronic_writing_device.png)](/wiki/File:Telautograph-1888_electronic_writing_device.png)

1888 [telautograph](/wiki/Telautograph "Telautograph") patent schema

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7f/2001interview.png/250px-2001interview.png)](/wiki/File:2001interview.png)

Wireless tablet device portrayed in the movie *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (1968)

The tablet computer and its associated operating system began with the development of [pen computing](/wiki/Pen_computing "Pen computing").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Electrical devices with [data input and output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") on a flat [information display](/wiki/Information_display "Information display") existed as early as 1888 with the [telautograph](/wiki/Telautograph "Telautograph"),[[11]](#cite_note-Gray-11) which used a sheet of paper as display and a pen attached to [electromechanical](/wiki/Electromechanical "Electromechanical") [actuators](/wiki/Actuator "Actuator"). Throughout the 20th century devices with these characteristics have been imagined and created whether as [blueprints](/wiki/Blueprint "Blueprint"), [prototypes](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype"), or [commercial products](/wiki/Commercial_product "Commercial product"). In addition to many academic and research systems, several companies released commercial products in the 1980s, with various input/output types tried out.

### Fictional and prototype tablets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Fictional and prototype tablets")]

Tablet computers appeared in a number of works of science fiction in the second half of the 20th century; all helped to promote and disseminate the concept to a wider audience.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Examples include:

* [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") described a Calculator Pad in his novel *[Foundation](/wiki/Foundation_Series "Foundation Series")* (1951)
* [Stanisław Lem](/wiki/Stanis%C5%82aw_Lem "Stanisław Lem") described the Opton in his novel *[Return from the Stars](/wiki/Return_from_the_Stars "Return from the Stars")* (1961)
* Numerous similar devices were depicted in [Gene Roddenberry](/wiki/Gene_Roddenberry "Gene Roddenberry")'s *[Star Trek: The Original Series](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series "Star Trek: The Original Series")* (1966)
* [Dr Who: The Dominators](/wiki/The_Dominators "The Dominators") Educator Balan holds a tablet which he inputs data into using swipe gestures (1967)[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Arthur C. Clarke](/wiki/Arthur_C._Clarke "Arthur C. Clarke")'s newspad[[14]](#cite_note-14) was depicted in [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick")'s film *[2001: A Space Odyssey](/wiki/2001:_A_Space_Odyssey_(film) "2001: A Space Odyssey (film)")* (1968)
* [Douglas Adams](/wiki/Douglas_Adams "Douglas Adams") described a tablet computer in *[The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy](/wiki/The_Hitchhiker%27s_Guide_to_the_Galaxy_(fictional) "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy (fictional)")* and the associated [comedy of the same name](/wiki/The_Hitchhiker%27s_Guide_to_the_Galaxy "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy") (1978)
* The science fiction TV series *[Star Trek: The Next Generation](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation "Star Trek: The Next Generation")* featured tablet computers which were designated as [PADDs](/wiki/PADD "PADD"), notable for (as with most computers in the show) using a touchscreen interface, both with and without a stylus (1987)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* A device more powerful than today's tablets appeared briefly in *[The Mote in God's Eye](/wiki/The_Mote_in_God%27s_Eye "The Mote in God's Eye")* (1974)[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* The [Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars") franchise features *datapads*, first described in print in the 1991 novel *[Heir to the Empire](/wiki/Heir_to_the_Empire "Heir to the Empire")*, and depicted on screen in the 1999 feature film, *[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")*

Further, real-life projects either proposed or created tablet computers, such as:

* In 1968, computer scientist [Alan Kay](/wiki/Alan_Kay "Alan Kay") envisioned a KiddiComp;[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) he developed and described the concept as a [Dynabook](/wiki/Dynabook "Dynabook") in his proposal, *A personal computer for children of all ages* (1972),[[19]](#cite_note-Dynabook-19) which outlines functionality similar to that supplied via a laptop computer, or (in some of its other incarnations) a tablet or slate computer, with the exception of near eternal battery life. The [target audience](/wiki/Target_audience "Target audience") was children.
* In 1979, the idea of a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") tablet that could detect an external force applied to one point on the screen was patented in Japan by a team at [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi "Hitachi") consisting of Masao Hotta, Yoshikazu Miyamoto, Norio Yokozawa and Yoshimitsu Oshima, who later received a US patent for their idea.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* In 1984, [Hartmut Esslinger](/wiki/Hartmut_Esslinger "Hartmut Esslinger") created a touchscreen [Macintosh](/wiki/Macintosh "Macintosh") concept, featuring a touch display and a keyboard, a prototype of which sits in the [San Francisco Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/San_Francisco_Museum_of_Modern_Art "San Francisco Museum of Modern Art")[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* In 1992, [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari") showed developers the Stylus, later renamed ST-Pad. The ST-Pad was based on the TOS/GEM [Atari ST](/wiki/Atari_ST "Atari ST") platform and prototyped early [handwriting recognition](/wiki/Handwriting_recognition "Handwriting recognition"). [Shiraz Shivji](/wiki/Shiraz_Shivji "Shiraz Shivji")'s company *Momentus* demonstrated in the same time a failed x86 [MS-DOS](/wiki/MS-DOS "MS-DOS") based Pen Computer with its own [graphical user interface](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") (GUI).[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* In 1994, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") initiated the [NewsPad](/wiki/NewsPad "NewsPad") project, inspired by Clarke and Kubrick's fictional work.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Acorn Computers](/wiki/NewsPad "NewsPad") developed and delivered an [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture")-based touch screen tablet computer for this program, branding it the "NewsPad"; the project ended in 1997.[[24]](#cite_note-RiscUser_v9i10-24)
* During the November 2000 COMDEX, [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") used the term [Tablet PC](/wiki/Microsoft_Tablet_PC "Microsoft Tablet PC") to describe a prototype handheld device they were demonstrating.[[25]](#cite_note-TabletPC,2000-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Microsoft_Tablet_PC_Overview-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Free_software_and_cellphones-27)
* In 2001, [Ericsson Mobile Communications](/wiki/Ericsson_Mobile_Communications "Ericsson Mobile Communications") announced an experimental product named the DelphiPad, which was developed in cooperation with the Centre for Wireless Communications in Singapore, with a touch-sensitive screen, [Netscape Navigator](/wiki/Netscape_Navigator "Netscape Navigator") as a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser"), and [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux") as its [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Early tablets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early tablets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Apple_Newton-IMG_0454-cropped.jpg/250px-Apple_Newton-IMG_0454-cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apple_Newton-IMG_0454-cropped.jpg)

[MessagePad](/wiki/MessagePad "MessagePad"), Apple's first produced tablet, released in 1993

Following earlier tablet computer products such as the [Pencept](/wiki/Pencept "Pencept") PenPad,[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) the [Linus Write-Top](/wiki/Linus_Write-Top "Linus Write-Top"),[[32]](#cite_note-computer-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Delbourg-Delphis_2024_168-33) and the CIC Handwriter,[[34]](#cite_note-34) in September 1989, [Grid Systems](/wiki/Grid_Systems "Grid Systems") released the first commercially successful tablet computer, the [GridPad](/wiki/GridPad "GridPad").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) All four products were based on extended versions of the [MS-DOS](/wiki/MS-DOS "MS-DOS") operating system. In 1992, IBM announced (in April) and shipped to developers (in October) the [ThinkPad 700T (2521)](/wiki/IBM_ThinkPad_700 "IBM ThinkPad 700"), which ran the [GO Corporation](/wiki/GO_Corporation "GO Corporation")'s [PenPoint OS](/wiki/PenPoint_OS "PenPoint OS"). Also based on PenPoint was [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T "AT&T")'s [EO Personal Communicator](/wiki/EO_Personal_Communicator "EO Personal Communicator") from 1993, which ran on AT&T's own hardware, including their own [AT&T Hobbit](/wiki/AT%26T_Hobbit "AT&T Hobbit") CPU. Apple Computer launched the [Apple Newton](/wiki/Apple_Newton "Apple Newton") personal digital assistant in 1993. It used Apple's own new [Newton OS](/wiki/Newton_OS "Newton OS"), initially running on hardware manufactured by Motorola and incorporating an [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") CPU, that Apple had specifically co-developed with [Acorn Computers](/wiki/Acorn_Computers "Acorn Computers"). The operating system and platform design were later licensed to [Sharp](/wiki/Sharp_Corporation "Sharp Corporation") and [Digital Ocean](/wiki/Digital_Ocean "Digital Ocean"), who went on to manufacture their own variants.

[Pen computing](/wiki/Pen_computing "Pen computing") was highly hyped by the media during the early 1990s. [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), the dominant PC software vendor, released [Windows for Pen Computing](/wiki/Windows_for_Pen_Computing "Windows for Pen Computing") in 1992 to compete against PenPoint OS. The company launched the WinPad project, working together with OEMs such as [Compaq](/wiki/Compaq "Compaq"), to create a small device with a [Windows](/wiki/Windows "Windows")-like operating system and handwriting recognition. However, the project was abandoned two years later; instead [Windows CE](/wiki/Windows_CE "Windows CE") was released in the form of "[Handheld PCs](/wiki/Handheld_PC "Handheld PC")" in 1996.[[37]](#cite_note-37) That year, [Palm, Inc.](/wiki/Palm,_Inc. "Palm, Inc.") released the first of the [Palm OS](/wiki/Palm_OS "Palm OS") based [PalmPilot](/wiki/PalmPilot "PalmPilot") touch and stylus based PDA, the touch based devices initially incorporating a [Motorola Dragonball](/wiki/Motorola_Dragonball "Motorola Dragonball") (68000) CPU. Also in 1996 Fujitsu released the Stylistic 1000 tablet format PC, running Microsoft [Windows 95](/wiki/Windows_95 "Windows 95"), on a 100 MHz AMD486 DX4 CPU, with 8 MB RAM offering stylus input, with the option of connecting a conventional Keyboard and mouse. Intel announced a [StrongARM](/wiki/StrongARM "StrongARM")[[38]](#cite_note-38) processor-based touchscreen tablet computer in 1999, under the name WebPAD. It was later re-branded as the "Intel Web Tablet".[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 2000, Norwegian company Screen Media AS and the German company Dosch & Amand Gmbh released the "FreePad".[[40]](#cite_note-FreePad-40) It was based on Linux and used the [Opera browser](/wiki/Opera_browser "Opera browser"). [Internet access](/wiki/Internet_access "Internet access") was provided by [DECT](/wiki/Digital_Enhanced_Cordless_Telecommunications "Digital Enhanced Cordless Telecommunications") DMAP, only available in Europe and provided up to 10 Mbit/s. The device had 16 MB storage, 32 MB of RAM and x86 compatible 166 MHz "Geode"-Microcontroller by [National Semiconductor](/wiki/National_Semiconductor "National Semiconductor").[[41]](#cite_note-41) The screen was 10.4" or 12.1" and was touch sensitive. It had slots for SIM cards to enable support of television set-up box. FreePad were sold in Norway and the Middle East; but the company was dissolved in 2003. [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony") released its Airboard tablet in Japan in late 2000 with full wireless Internet capabilities.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Fujitsu_Siemens_Lifebook_T3010.jpg/250px-Fujitsu_Siemens_Lifebook_T3010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fujitsu_Siemens_Lifebook_T3010.jpg)

A [Fujitsu Siemens](/wiki/Fujitsu_Siemens_Computers "Fujitsu Siemens Computers") Lifebook tablet running [Windows XP](/wiki/Windows_XP "Windows XP"), released in 2003

In the late 1990s, Microsoft launched the [Handheld PC](/wiki/Handheld_PC "Handheld PC") platform using their Windows CE operating system; while most devices were not tablets, a few touch enabled tablets were released on the platform such as the [Fujitsu](/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu") PenCentra 130 or [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens")'s [SIMpad](/wiki/SIMpad "SIMpad").[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Microsoft took a more significant approach to tablets in 2002 as it attempted to define the [Microsoft Tablet PC](/wiki/Microsoft_Tablet_PC "Microsoft Tablet PC")[[46]](#cite_note-markoff-46) as a mobile computer for field work in business,[[47]](#cite_note-earthweb-47) though their devices failed, mainly due to pricing and [usability](/wiki/Usability "Usability") decisions that limited them to their original purpose – such as the existing devices being too heavy to be held with one hand for extended periods, and having legacy applications created for desktop interfaces and not well adapted to the slate format.[[48]](#cite_note-BallStillDoesnt2010-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/N800_frontside2.jpg/250px-N800_frontside2.jpg)](/wiki/File:N800_frontside2.jpg)

The [Nokia N800](/wiki/Nokia_N800 "Nokia N800"), the second tablet manufactured by [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia")

[Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") had plans for an Internet tablet since before 2000. An early model was test manufactured in 2001, the Nokia M510, which was running on [EPOC](/wiki/EPOC_(operating_system) "EPOC (operating system)") and featuring an [Opera](/wiki/Opera_(browser) "Opera (browser)") browser, speakers and a 10-inch 800×600 screen, but it was not released because of fears that the market was not ready for it.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Nokia entered the tablet space in May 2005 with the [Nokia 770](/wiki/Nokia_770_Internet_Tablet "Nokia 770 Internet Tablet") running [Maemo](/wiki/Maemo "Maemo"), a Debian-based Linux distribution custom-made for their [Internet tablet](/wiki/Internet_tablet "Internet tablet") line. The [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") and [application framework](/wiki/Application_framework "Application framework") layer, named [Hildon](/wiki/Hildon "Hildon"), was an early instance of a [software platform](/wiki/Software_platform "Software platform") for generic computing in a tablet device intended for internet consumption.[[50]](#cite_note-50) But Nokia did not commit to it as their only platform for their future mobile devices and the project competed against other in-house platforms and later replaced it with the [Series 60](/wiki/Series_60 "Series 60").[[51]](#cite_note-51) Nokia used the term *internet tablet* to refer to a portable [information appliance](/wiki/Information_appliance "Information appliance") that focused on Internet use and media consumption, in the range between a [personal digital assistant](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant") (PDA) and an [Ultra-Mobile PC](/wiki/Ultra-Mobile_PC "Ultra-Mobile PC") (UMPC). They made two mobile phones, the N900 that runs Maemo, and N9 that run Meego.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Before the release of iPad, Axiotron introduced[[53]](#cite_note-modbookRelease-53) an aftermarket, heavily modified Apple [MacBook](/wiki/MacBook "MacBook") called [Modbook](/wiki/Axiotron_Modbook "Axiotron Modbook"), a [Mac OS X](/wiki/Mac_OS_X "Mac OS X")-based tablet computer. The Modbook uses Apple's [Inkwell](/wiki/Inkwell_(Macintosh) "Inkwell (Macintosh)") for handwriting and gesture recognition, and uses digitization hardware from [Wacom](/wiki/Wacom_(company) "Wacom (company)"). To get Mac OS X to talk to the digitizer on the integrated tablet, the Modbook was supplied with a third-party driver.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

Following the launch of the [Ultra-mobile PC](/wiki/Ultra-mobile_PC "Ultra-mobile PC"), Intel began the [Mobile Internet Device](/wiki/Mobile_Internet_Device "Mobile Internet Device") initiative, which took the same hardware and combined it with a tabletized Linux configuration. Intel codeveloped the lightweight [Moblin](/wiki/Moblin "Moblin") (mobile Linux) operating system following the successful launch of the Atom CPU series on netbooks. In 2010, Nokia and [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") combined the Maemo and Moblin projects to form MeeGo, a Linux-based operating system supports netbooks and tablets. The first tablet using MeeGo was the [Neofonie](/wiki/Neofonie "Neofonie") [WeTab](/wiki/WeTab "WeTab") launched September 2010 in Germany. The WeTab used an extended version of the MeeGo operating system called WeTab OS. WeTab OS adds runtimes for Android and [Adobe AIR](/wiki/Adobe_AIR "Adobe AIR") and provides a proprietary user interface optimized for the WeTab device. On September 27, 2011, the [Linux Foundation](/wiki/Linux_Foundation "Linux Foundation") announced that MeeGo would be replaced in 2012 by [Tizen](/wiki/Tizen "Tizen").[[55]](#cite_note-next-55)

### Modern tablets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern tablets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Steve_Jobs_with_the_Apple_iPad_no_logo.jpg/250px-Steve_Jobs_with_the_Apple_iPad_no_logo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steve_Jobs_with_the_Apple_iPad_no_logo.jpg)

[Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs "Steve Jobs") introducing the [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad") in San Francisco on January 27, 2010

Android was the first of the 2000s-era dominating platforms for tablet computers to reach the market. In 2008, the first plans for Android-based tablets appeared. The first products were released in 2009. Among them was the Archos 5, a pocket-sized model with a 5-inch [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen"), that was first released with a proprietary operating system and later (in 2009) released with Android 1.4. The Camangi WebStation was released in Q2 2009. The first [LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)") [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") tablet appeared late 2009 and was made by ICD for [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon "Verizon"). This unit was called the Ultra, but a version called Vega was released around the same time. Ultra had a 7-inch display while Vega's was 15 inches. Many more products followed in 2010. Several manufacturers waited for [Android Honeycomb](/wiki/Android_Honeycomb "Android Honeycomb"), specifically adapted for use with tablets, which debuted in February 2011.

[Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") is often credited for defining a new class of consumer device with the [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"),[[56]](#cite_note-infoworld-56) which shaped the commercial market for tablets in the following years,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and was the most successful tablet at the time of its release. Its debut in 2010 pushed tablets into the mainstream.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung")'s [Galaxy Tab](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Tab "Samsung Galaxy Tab") and others followed, continuing the trends towards the features listed above. iPads and competing devices were tested by the U.S. military in 2011[[60]](#cite_note-60) and cleared for secure use in 2013.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In March 2012, *[PC Magazine](/wiki/PC_Magazine "PC Magazine")* reported that 31% of U.S. Internet users owned a tablet, used mainly for viewing published content such as video and news.[[62]](#cite_note-USownership-62) The top-selling line of devices was Apple's iPad with 100 million sold between its release in April 2010 and mid-October 2012,[[63]](#cite_note-iPadSales-63) but iPad [market share](#Sales) (number of units) dropped to 36% in 2013 with [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") tablets climbing to 62%. Android tablet sales volume was 121 million devices, plus 52 million, between 2012 and 2013 respectively.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Individual brands of Android operating system devices or compatibles follow iPad with Amazon's [Kindle Fire](/wiki/Kindle_Fire "Kindle Fire") with 7 million, and Barnes & Noble's [Nook](/wiki/Nook_tablet "Nook tablet") with 5 million.[[65]](#cite_note-TabletSales1012-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Android_os.png/250px-Android_os.png)](/wiki/File:Android_os.png)

[Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics")'s [Galaxy Tab](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Tab "Samsung Galaxy Tab") tablet PC

The [BlackBerry PlayBook](/wiki/BlackBerry_PlayBook "BlackBerry PlayBook") was announced in September 2010 that ran the [BlackBerry Tablet OS](/wiki/BlackBerry_Tablet_OS "BlackBerry Tablet OS").[[68]](#cite_note-68) The BlackBerry PlayBook was officially released to US and Canadian consumers on April 19, 2011. [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") announced that the [TouchPad](/wiki/HP_TouchPad "HP TouchPad"), running [WebOS](/wiki/WebOS "WebOS") 3.0 on a 1.2 GHz [Qualcomm Snapdragon](/wiki/Qualcomm_Snapdragon "Qualcomm Snapdragon") CPU, would be released in June 2011. On August 18, 2011, HP announced the discontinuation of the TouchPad, due to sluggish sales.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In 2013, the [Mozilla Foundation](/wiki/Mozilla_Foundation "Mozilla Foundation") announced a prototype tablet model with [Foxconn](/wiki/Foxconn "Foxconn") which ran on [Firefox OS](/wiki/Firefox_OS "Firefox OS").[[70]](#cite_note-thevergefirefoxos-70) Firefox OS was discontinued in 2016.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The [Canonical](/wiki/Canonical_Ltd "Canonical Ltd") hinted that [Ubuntu](/wiki/Ubuntu_OS "Ubuntu OS") would be available on tablets by 2014.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In February 2016, there was a commercial release of the [BQ Aquaris Ubuntu tablet](/wiki/BQ_Aquaris_M10#Ubuntu_Edition "BQ Aquaris M10") using the [Ubuntu Touch](/wiki/Ubuntu_Touch "Ubuntu Touch") operating system.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Canonical terminated support for the project due to lack of market interest on April 5, 2017[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) and it was then adopted by the UBports as a community project.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

As of February 2014, 83% of mobile app developers were targeting tablets,[[77]](#cite_note-77) but 93% of developers were targeting smartphones. By 2014, around 23% of B2B companies were said to have deployed tablets for sales-related activities, according to a survey report by Corporate Visions.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The iPad held majority use in North America, Western Europe, Japan, Australia, and most of the Americas. Android tablets were more popular in most of Asia (China and Russia an exception), Africa and Eastern Europe. In 2015 tablet sales did not increase. Apple remained the largest seller but its market share declined below 25%.[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") vice president Gary Riding said early in 2016 that tablets were only doing well among those using them for work. Newer models were more expensive and designed for a keyboard and stylus, which reflected the changing uses.[[80]](#cite_note-80) As of early 2016, Android reigned over the market with 65%. Apple took the number 2 spot with 26%, and Windows took a distant third with the remaining 9%.[[81]](#cite_note-mspoweruser.com-81) In 2018, out of 4.4 billion computing devices Android accounted for 2 billion, iOS for 1 billion, and the remainder were PCs, in various forms (desktop, notebook, or tablet), running various operating systems (Windows, macOS, ChromeOS, Linux, etc.).[[82]](#cite_note-2018distribution-82)

Since the early 2020s, various companies have used [foldable technology](/wiki/Foldable_smartphone "Foldable smartphone") to create tablet computers.[[83]](#cite_note-83) [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics")'s line of [Samsung Galaxy Z Fold](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Z_series#Z_Fold "Samsung Galaxy Z series") phones are able to unfold into small tablets, and companies like [Asus](/wiki/Asus "Asus") and [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") created large 12 and 17-inch class tablets which can fold into a smaller footprint for use as a laptop.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Laplet%2C_Phablet_and_Cricket.jpg/330px-Laplet%2C_Phablet_and_Cricket.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laplet,_Phablet_and_Cricket.jpg)

Crossover tablet device types from 2014: [Microsoft Surface Pro 3](/wiki/Microsoft_Surface_Pro_3 "Microsoft Surface Pro 3") [laplet](/wiki/Laplet "Laplet") and [Sony Xperia Z Ultra](/wiki/Sony_Xperia_Z_Ultra "Sony Xperia Z Ultra") [phablet](/wiki/Phablet "Phablet"), next to a generic blue lighter for size comparison

Tablets can be loosely grouped into several categories by physical size, kind of operating system installed, input and output technology, and uses.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Slate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Slate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/IPad_mini_Air_Pro.jpeg/250px-IPad_mini_Air_Pro.jpeg)](/wiki/File:IPad_mini_Air_Pro.jpeg)

[iPad mini](/wiki/IPad_mini "IPad mini"), [iPad Air](/wiki/IPad_Air "IPad Air"), [iPad Pro](/wiki/IPad_Pro "IPad Pro")

The size of a slate varies, but slates begin at 6 inches (approximately 15 cm).[[85]](#cite_note-85) Some models in the larger than 10-inch (25 cm) category include the [Samsung Galaxy Tab Pro 12.2](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Tab_Pro_12.2 "Samsung Galaxy Tab Pro 12.2") at 12.2 inches (31 cm), the Toshiba Excite at 13.3 inches (33 cm)[[86]](#cite_note-86) and the Dell XPS 18 at 18.4 inches (47 cm).[[87]](#cite_note-87) As of March 2013, the thinnest tablet on the market was the [Sony Xperia Tablet Z](/wiki/Sony_Xperia_Tablet_Z "Sony Xperia Tablet Z") at only 0.27 inches (6.9 mm) thick.[[88]](#cite_note-88) On September 9, 2015, Apple released the [iPad Pro](/wiki/IPad_Pro "IPad Pro") with a 12.9 inches (33 cm) screen size, larger than the regular [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad").[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Mini tablet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mini tablet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/1st_gen_Comparison_iPad_Mini_%26_Google_Nexus_7_%26_Kindle_Fire_Wikipedia_screen_03_2013_6262.jpg/250px-1st_gen_Comparison_iPad_Mini_%26_Google_Nexus_7_%26_Kindle_Fire_Wikipedia_screen_03_2013_6262.jpg)](/wiki/File:1st_gen_Comparison_iPad_Mini_%26_Google_Nexus_7_%26_Kindle_Fire_Wikipedia_screen_03_2013_6262.jpg)

Comparison of several mini tablet computers: [Amazon Kindle Fire](/wiki/Amazon_Kindle_Fire "Amazon Kindle Fire") (left), [iPad Mini](/wiki/IPad_Mini "IPad Mini") (center), and [Google Nexus 7](/wiki/Google_Nexus_7 "Google Nexus 7") (right)

Mini tablets are smaller and weigh less than slates, with typical screen sizes between 7–8 inches (18–20 cm). The first commercially successful mini tablets were introduced by [Amazon.com](/wiki/Amazon.com "Amazon.com") ([Kindle Fire](/wiki/Amazon_Kindle_Fire "Amazon Kindle Fire")), [Barnes & Noble](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble "Barnes & Noble") ([Nook Tablet](/wiki/Nook_Tablet "Nook Tablet")), and [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") ([Galaxy Tab](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Tab "Samsung Galaxy Tab")) in 2011; and by Google ([Nexus 7](/wiki/Nexus_7_(2012_version) "Nexus 7 (2012 version)")) in 2012. They operate identically to ordinary tablets but have lower specifications compared to them.

On September 14, 2012, Amazon, Inc. released an upgraded version of the Kindle Fire, the [Kindle Fire HD](/wiki/Kindle_Fire_HD "Kindle Fire HD"), with higher screen resolution and more features compared to its predecessor, yet remaining only 7 inches.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In October 2012, Apple released the [iPad Mini](/wiki/IPad_Mini "IPad Mini") with a 7.9-inch screen size, about 2 inches smaller than the regular [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"), but less powerful than the then current [iPad 3](/wiki/IPad_3 "IPad 3").[[91]](#cite_note-91) On July 24, 2013, Google released an upgraded version of the [Nexus 7](/wiki/Nexus_7_(2013_version) "Nexus 7 (2013 version)"), with FHD display, dual cameras, stereo speakers, more color accuracy, performance improvement, built-in [wireless charging](/wiki/Qi_(inductive_power_standard) "Qi (inductive power standard)"), and a variant with 4G LTE support for AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon. In September 2013, Amazon further updated the Fire tablet with the [Kindle Fire HDX](/wiki/Kindle_Fire_HDX "Kindle Fire HDX"). In November 2013, Apple released the [iPad Mini 2](/wiki/IPad_Mini_2 "IPad Mini 2"), which remained at 7.9 inches and nearly matched the hardware of the [iPad Air](/wiki/IPad_Air "IPad Air").

### Phablet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Phablet")]

Main article: [Phablet](/wiki/Phablet "Phablet")

[Smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and tablets are similar devices, differentiated by the former typically having smaller screens and most tablets lacking [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network") capability. Since 2010, crossover touchscreen smartphones with screens larger than 5 inches have been released. That size is generally considered larger than a traditional smartphone, creating the hybrid category of the *[phablet](/wiki/Phablet "Phablet")* by [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")[[92]](#cite_note-92) and other publications. "Phablet" is a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of "phone" and "tablet".

At the time of the introduction of the first phablets, they had screens of 5.3 to 5.5 inches, but as of 2017 screen sizes up to 5.5 inches are considered typical. Examples of phablets from 2017 and onward are the [Samsung Galaxy Note series](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_series "Samsung Galaxy Note series") (newer models of 5.7 inches), the [LG V10](/wiki/LG_V10 "LG V10")/[V20](/wiki/LG_V20 "LG V20") (5.7 inches), the [Sony Xperia XA Ultra](/wiki/Sony_Xperia_XA_Ultra "Sony Xperia XA Ultra") (6 inches), the [Huawei Mate 9](/wiki/Huawei_Mate_9 "Huawei Mate 9") (5.9 inches), and the Huawei Honor (MediaPad) X2 (7 inches).

### 2-in-1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2-in-1")]

Main article: [2-in-1 PC](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Surface_pro.jpeg/250px-Surface_pro.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Surface_pro.jpeg)

[Microsoft Surface Pro 11](/wiki/Surface_Pro_(11th_generation) "Surface Pro (11th generation)"), a prominent 2-in-1 detachable tablet

A 2-in-1 PC is a hybrid or combination of a tablet and laptop computer that has features of both. Distinct from tablets, [2-in-1 PCs](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC") all have physical [keyboards](/wiki/Keyboard_(computing) "Keyboard (computing)"), but they are either concealable by folding them back and under the touchscreen ("2-in-1 convertible") or detachable ("2-in-1 detachable"). 2-in-1s typically also can display a virtual keyboard on their touchscreens when their physical keyboards are concealed or detached. Some 2-in-1s have processors and [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") like those of laptops, such as [Windows 10](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10"), while having the flexibility of operation as a tablet. Further, 2-in-1s may have typical laptop [I/O](/wiki/I/O "I/O") ports, such as [USB 3](/wiki/USB_3 "USB 3") and [DisplayPort](/wiki/DisplayPort "DisplayPort"), and may connect to traditional [PC](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") [peripheral devices](/wiki/Peripheral_devices "Peripheral devices") and external displays. Simple tablets are mainly used as [media consumption](/wiki/Media_consumption "Media consumption") devices, while 2-in-1s have capacity for both [media consumption](/wiki/Media_consumption "Media consumption") and [content creation](/wiki/Content_creation "Content creation"), and thus 2-in-1s are often called *laptop* or *[desktop replacement computers](/wiki/Desktop_replacement_computer "Desktop replacement computer")*.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

There are two species of 2-in-1s:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Asus_Transformer_Pad_TF701T_Tablet_and_Keyboard_Dock.png/250px-Asus_Transformer_Pad_TF701T_Tablet_and_Keyboard_Dock.png)](/wiki/File:Asus_Transformer_Pad_TF701T_Tablet_and_Keyboard_Dock.png)

[Asus Transformer Pad](/wiki/ASUS_Transformer "ASUS Transformer"), a 2-in-1 detachable tablet, powered by the [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system")

* **Convertibles** have a [chassis](/wiki/Chassis#Electronics "Chassis") design by which their physical keyboard may be concealed by flipping/folding the keyboard behind the chassis. Examples include 2-in-1 PCs of the [Lenovo Yoga](/wiki/Lenovo_Yoga "Lenovo Yoga") series.
* **Detachables** or **Hybrids** have physical keyboards that may be detached from their [chassis](/wiki/Chassis#Electronics "Chassis"), even while the 2-in-1 is operating. Examples include 2-in-1 PCs of the [Asus Transformer](/wiki/Asus_Transformer "Asus Transformer") Pad and Book series, the [iPad Pro](/wiki/IPad_Pro "IPad Pro"), and the Microsoft [Surface Book](/wiki/Surface_Book "Surface Book") and [Surface Pro](/wiki/Microsoft_Surface "Microsoft Surface").

### Gaming tablet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Gaming tablet")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Nvidia_Shield_Tablet.jpg/250px-Nvidia_Shield_Tablet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nvidia_Shield_Tablet.jpg)

Nvidia Shield Tablet, notable gaming tablet

Some tablets are modified by adding physical [gamepad](/wiki/Gamepad "Gamepad") buttons such as [D-pad](/wiki/D-pad "D-pad") and thumb sticks for better gaming experience combined with the [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") and all other features of a typical tablet computer. Most of these tablets are targeted to run native OS games and [emulator](/wiki/Emulator "Emulator") games. [Nvidia](/wiki/Nvidia "Nvidia")'s [Shield Tablet](/wiki/Shield_Tablet "Shield Tablet"), with an 8-inch (200 mm) display, and running [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), is an example. It runs Android games purchased from [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") store. [PC games](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") can also be [streamed](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") to the tablet from computers with some higher end models of Nvidia-powered [video cards](/wiki/Video_card "Video card"). The [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") hybrid console and its successor [Nintendo Switch 2](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_2 "Nintendo Switch 2") are also a gaming tablet that runs on [its own system software](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_system_software "Nintendo Switch system software"), features detachable [Joy-Con](/wiki/Joy-Con "Joy-Con") controllers with motion controls (along with its successor [Joy-Con 2](/wiki/Joy-Con "Joy-Con") controllers to additionally consist with the mouse control, replacing the IR motion camera sensor) and three gaming modes: table-top mode using its kickstand, traditional docked/TV mode and handheld mode. While not entirely an actual tablet form factor due to their sizes, some other handheld console including the smaller version of Nintendo Switch and its successor Nintendo Switch 2, the [Nintendo Switch Lite](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_Lite "Nintendo Switch Lite"), and [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita") are treated as a gaming tablet or tablet replacement by community and reviewer/publisher due to their capabilities on browsing the internet and multimedia capabilities.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Booklet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Booklet")]

Booklets are [dual-touchscreen](/wiki/Dual-touchscreen "Dual-touchscreen") tablet computers with a [clamshell](/wiki/Flip_(form) "Flip (form)") design that can fold like a laptop. Examples include the [Microsoft Courier](/wiki/Microsoft_Courier "Microsoft Courier"), which was discontinued in 2010,[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) the [Sony Tablet P](/wiki/Sony_Tablet_P "Sony Tablet P") (considered a flop),[[97]](#cite_note-97) and the [Toshiba Libretto W100](/wiki/Toshiba_Libretto_W100 "Toshiba Libretto W100").

### Customized business tablet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Customized business tablet")]

Customized business tablets are built specifically for a business customer's particular needs from a hardware and software perspective, and delivered in a [business-to-business](/wiki/Business-to-business "Business-to-business") transaction. For example, in hardware, a transportation company may find that the consumer-grade [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") module in an [off-the-shelf](/wiki/Commercial_off-the-shelf "Commercial off-the-shelf") tablet provides insufficient accuracy, so a tablet can be customized and embedded with a professional-grade antenna to provide a better GPS signal. Such tablets may also be [ruggedized](/wiki/Rugged_computer "Rugged computer") for field use. For a software example, the same transportation company might remove certain software functions in the Android system, such as the web browser, to reduce costs from needless cellular network data consumption of an employee, and add custom package management software. Other applications may call for a [resistive touchscreen](/wiki/Resistive_touchscreen "Resistive touchscreen") and other special hardware and software.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Ziosk_games.jpg/250px-Ziosk_games.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ziosk_games.jpg)

Games on a Ziosk table ordering tablet at an Olive Garden restaurant

A table ordering tablet is a touchscreen tablet computer designed for use in [casual restaurants](/wiki/Types_of_restaurant#Casual_dining "Types of restaurant").[[98]](#cite_note-98) Such devices allow users to order food and drinks, play games and pay their bill. Since 2013, [restaurant chains](/wiki/Chain_store "Chain store") including [Chili's](/wiki/Chili%27s "Chili's"),[[99]](#cite_note-99) [Olive Garden](/wiki/Olive_Garden "Olive Garden")[[100]](#cite_note-100) and [Red Robin](/wiki/Red_Robin "Red Robin")[[101]](#cite_note-101) have adopted them. As of 2014, the two most popular brands were [Ziosk](/wiki/Ziosk "Ziosk") and [Presto](/w/index.php?title=Presto_Tablet&action=edit&redlink=1 "Presto Tablet (page does not exist)").[[102]](#cite_note-102) The devices have been criticized by servers who claim that some restaurants determine their hours based on customer feedback in areas unrelated to service.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### E-reader

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: E-reader")]

Any device that can display text on a screen may act as an [E-reader](/wiki/E-reader "E-reader"). While traditionally E-readers are designed primarily for the purpose of reading digital e-books and periodicals, modern E-readers that use a mobile operating system such as Android have incorporated modern functionally including internet browsing and multimedia capabilities; for example the Huawei MatePad Paper is a tablet that uses e-ink instead of a typical LCD or LED panel, hence focusing on the reading of digital content while maintaining the internet and multimedia capabilities. Some E-readers such as the PocketBook InkPad Color and the ONYX BOOX NOVA 3 Color even came with a colored e-ink panel and speaker which allowed for a higher degree of multimedia consumption and video playback.

The [Kindle line](/wiki/Amazon_Kindle "Amazon Kindle") from Amazon was originally limited to E-reading capabilities; however, an update to their Kindle firmware added the ability to browse the Internet and play audio, allowing Kindles to be alternatives to a traditional tablet, in some cases, with a more readable e-ink panel and greater battery life, and providing the user with access to wider multimedia capabilities compared to the older model.

## Hardware

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hardware")]

### System architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: System architecture")]

See also: [Comparison of tablet computers](/wiki/Comparison_of_tablet_computers "Comparison of tablet computers")

Two major architectures dominate the tablet market,[[104]](#cite_note-104) [ARM Ltd.](/wiki/ARM_Ltd. "ARM Ltd.")'s [ARM architecture](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") and Intel's and AMD's [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86"). Intel's x86, including [x86-64](/wiki/X86-64 "X86-64") has powered the "IBM compatible" PC since 1981 and Apple's Macintosh computers since 2006. The CPUs have been incorporated into tablet PCs over the years and generally offer greater performance along with the ability to run full versions of Microsoft [Windows](/wiki/Windows "Windows"), along with Windows desktop and enterprise applications. Non-Windows based x86 tablets include the [JooJoo](/wiki/JooJoo "JooJoo"). Intel announced plans to enter the tablet market with its [Atom](/wiki/Intel_Atom "Intel Atom") in 2010.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) In October 2013, Intel's foundry operation announced plans to build FPGA-based quad cores for ARM and x86 processors.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

ARM has been the CPU architecture of choice for manufacturers of smartphones (95% ARM), PDAs, digital cameras (80% ARM), [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), DSL routers, smart televisions (70% ARM), storage devices and tablet computers (95% ARM).[[108]](#cite_note-108)[*[independent source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Independent_sources "Wikipedia:Independent sources")*] This dominance began with the release of the mobile-focused and comparatively power-efficient 32-bit ARM610 processor originally designed for the [Apple Newton](/wiki/Apple_Newton "Apple Newton") in 1993 and ARM3-using Acorn A4 laptop in 1992. The chip was adopted by Psion, Palm and Nokia for PDAs and later smartphones, camera phones, cameras, etc. ARM's licensing model supported this success by allowing device manufacturers to license, alter and fabricate custom SoC derivatives tailored to their own products. This has helped manufacturers extend battery life and shrink component count along with the size of devices.

The multiple licensees ensured that multiple fabricators could supply near-identical products, while encouraging price competition. This forced unit prices down to a fraction of their x86 equivalents. The architecture has historically had limited support from Microsoft, with only [Windows CE](/wiki/Windows_CE "Windows CE") available, but with the 2012 release of Windows 8, Microsoft announced added support for the architecture, shipping their own ARM-based tablet computer, branded the [Microsoft Surface](/wiki/Microsoft_Surface "Microsoft Surface"), as well as an x86-64 Intel Core i5 variant branded as Microsoft Surface Pro.[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) Intel tablet chip sales were 1 million units in 2012, and 12 million units in 2013.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Intel chairman Andy Bryant has stated that its 2014 goal is to quadruple its tablet chip sales to 40 million units by the end of that year,[[114]](#cite_note-114) as an investment for 2015.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

See also: [Mobile Internet Device § Intel MID Platforms](/wiki/Mobile_Internet_Device#Intel_MID_Platforms "Mobile Internet Device")

### Display

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Display")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_18.JPG/250px-IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_18.JPG)](/wiki/File:IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_18.JPG)

[Samsung Galaxy Tab](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Tab "Samsung Galaxy Tab") demonstrating [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch")

A key component among tablet computers is touch input on a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") display. This allows the user to navigate easily and type with a [virtual keyboard](/wiki/Virtual_keyboard "Virtual keyboard") on the screen or press other icons on the screen to open apps or files. The first tablet to do this was the [Linus Write-Top](/wiki/Linus_Write-Top "Linus Write-Top") by [Linus Technologies](/wiki/Linus_Technologies "Linus Technologies"); the tablet featured both a [stylus](/wiki/Stylus "Stylus"), a pen-like tool to aid with precision in a touchscreen device, as well as handwriting recognition.[[32]](#cite_note-computer-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Delbourg-Delphis_2024_168-33) The system must respond to on-screen touches rather than clicks of a keyboard or mouse. This operation makes precise use of our [eye–hand coordination](/wiki/Eye%E2%80%93hand_coordination "Eye–hand coordination").[[119]](#cite_note-119)[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)

Touchscreens usually come in one of two forms:

* [Resistive touchscreens](/wiki/Resistive_touchscreen "Resistive touchscreen") are passive and respond to pressure on the screen. They allow a high level of [precision](/wiki/Accuracy_and_precision "Accuracy and precision"), useful in emulating a [pointer](/wiki/Pointer_(computing_WIMP) "Pointer (computing WIMP)") (as is common in tablet computers) but may require calibration. Because of the high resolution, a stylus or fingernail is often used. Stylus-oriented systems are less suited to [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch").
* [Capacitive touchscreens](/wiki/Capacitive_sensing "Capacitive sensing") tend to be less accurate, but more responsive than resistive devices. Because they require a conductive material, such as a fingertip, for input, they are not common among stylus-oriented devices but are prominent on consumer devices. Most finger-driven capacitive screens do not currently support pressure input (except for the [iPhone 6S](/wiki/IPhone_6S "IPhone 6S") and later models), but some tablets use a pressure-sensitive stylus or [active pen](/wiki/Active_pen "Active pen").[[122]](#cite_note-122)
* Some tablets can recognize individual palms, while some professional-grade tablets use pressure-sensitive films, such as those on [graphics tablets](/wiki/Graphics_tablet "Graphics tablet"). Some capacitive touch-screens can detect the size of the touched area and the pressure used.[[123]](#cite_note-buxtonoverview-123)

Since mid-2010s, most tablets use [capacitive](/wiki/Capacitive_sensing "Capacitive sensing") [touchscreens](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") with [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch"), unlike earlier [resistive touchscreen](/wiki/Resistive_touchscreen "Resistive touchscreen") devices which users needed styluses to perform inputs.

There are also [electronic paper](/wiki/Electronic_paper "Electronic paper") tablets such as [Sony Digital Paper DPTS1](/wiki/Sony_Digital_Paper_DPTS1 "Sony Digital Paper DPTS1") and [reMarkable](/wiki/ReMarkable "ReMarkable") that use [E ink](/wiki/E_ink "E ink") for its display technology.

### Handwriting recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Handwriting recognition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/%E4%BA%BA-red.png/250px-%E4%BA%BA-red.png)](/wiki/File:%E4%BA%BA-red.png)

Chinese characters like this one meaning "person" can be written by handwriting recognition ([![人 animation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/%E4%BA%BA-order.gif/20px-%E4%BA%BA-order.gif)](/wiki/File:%E4%BA%BA-order.gif "人 animation"), [Mandarin](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): *rén*, [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): *in*, Japanese: *jin*, *nin*; *hito*, [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"): jan4). The character has two strokes, the first shown here in brown, and the second in red. The black area represents the starting position of the writing instrument.

Many tablets support a stylus and support [handwriting recognition](/wiki/Handwriting_recognition "Handwriting recognition"). [Wacom](/wiki/Wacom_(company) "Wacom (company)") and [N-trig](/wiki/N-trig "N-trig") digital pens provide approximately 2500 DPI resolution for handwriting, exceeding the resolution of capacitive touch screens by more than a factor of 10. These pens also support pressure sensitivity, allowing for "variable-width stroke-based" characters, such as Chinese/Japanese/Korean writing, due to their built-in capability of "pressure sensing". Pressure is also used in digital art applications such as [Autodesk Sketchbook](/wiki/Autodesk_Sketchbook "Autodesk Sketchbook").[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) Apps exist on both iOS and Android platforms for handwriting recognition and in 2015 Google introduced its own handwriting input with support for 82 languages.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Other features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other features")]

After 2007, with access to capacitive screens and the success of the iPhone, other features became common, such as multi-touch features (in which the user can touch the screen in multiple places to trigger actions) and other [natural user interface](/wiki/Natural_user_interface "Natural user interface") features, as well as [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory") [solid state storage](/wiki/Solid_state_storage "Solid state storage") and "instant on" [warm-booting](/wiki/Warm_boot "Warm boot"); external [USB](/wiki/USB "USB") and [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") [keyboards](/wiki/Computer_keyboard "Computer keyboard") defined tablets.

Most tablets released since mid-2010 use a version of an [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") processor for longer battery life. The [ARM Cortex family](/wiki/ARM_Cortex-A9_MPCore "ARM Cortex-A9 MPCore") is powerful enough for tasks such as [internet browsing](/wiki/Mobile_browser "Mobile browser"), light creative and production work and [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game").[[127]](#cite_note-The_Coming_War:_ARM_versus_x86-127)

Other features are: High-definition, [anti-glare display](/wiki/Glossy_display "Glossy display"), touchscreen, lower weight and longer [battery life](/wiki/Battery_(electricity)#battery_lifetime "Battery (electricity)") than a comparably-sized laptop, wireless local area and internet connectivity (usually with [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") standard and optional [mobile broadband](/wiki/Mobile_broadband "Mobile broadband")), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth") for connecting peripherals and communicating with local devices, ports for wired connections and charging, for example [USB ports](/wiki/USB_port "USB port"), Early devices had IR support and could work as a TV [remote controller](/wiki/Remote_controller "Remote controller"), [docking station](/wiki/Docking_station "Docking station"), keyboard and added connectivity, on-board [flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory"), ports for removable storage, various [cloud storage](/wiki/Cloud_storage "Cloud storage") services for backup and [syncing](/wiki/File_synchronization "File synchronization") data across devices, local storage on a [local area network](/wiki/Local_area_network "Local area network") (LAN).

* [Speech recognition](/wiki/Speech_recognition "Speech recognition") Google introduced voice input in [Android 2.1](/wiki/Android_2.1 "Android 2.1") in 2009 and [voice actions](/wiki/Google_Voice_Search "Google Voice Search") in [2.2](/wiki/Android_Froyo "Android Froyo") in 2010, with up to five languages (now around 40).[[128]](#cite_note-128) [Siri](/wiki/Siri "Siri") was introduced as a system-wide personal assistant on the [iPhone 4S](/wiki/IPhone_4S "IPhone 4S") in 2011 and now supports nearly 20 languages. In both cases, the voice input is sent to central servers to perform general speech recognition and thus requires a network connection for more than simple commands.
* [Near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication") with other compatible devices including [ISO/IEC 14443](/wiki/ISO/IEC_14443 "ISO/IEC 14443") RFID tags.

## Software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Software")]

### Current tablet operating systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Current tablet operating systems")]

Tablets, like conventional PCs, use several different [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system"), though [dual-booting](/wiki/Multi_boot "Multi boot") is rare. Tablet operating systems come in two classes:

* [Desktop computer](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") operating systems
* [Mobile operating systems](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system")

Desktop OS-based tablets are currently thicker and heavier. They require more storage and [more cooling](/wiki/CPU_power_dissipation "CPU power dissipation") and give less battery life. They can run processor-intensive graphical applications in addition to [mobile apps](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app"), and have more [ports](/wiki/Computer_port_(hardware) "Computer port (hardware)").[[129]](#cite_note-129)

Mobile-based tablets are the reverse, and run only mobile apps. They can use battery life conservatively because the processor is significantly smaller. This allows the battery to last much longer than the common laptop.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In Q1 2018, Android tablets had 62% of the market, Apple's iOS had 23.4% of the market and Windows 10 had 14.6% of the market.[[131]](#cite_note-auto-131) In late 2021, iOS has 55% use worldwide (varies by continent, e.g. below 50% in South America and Africa) and Android 45% use. Still, Android tablets have more use than iOS in virtually all countries, except for e.g. the U.S. and China.[[132]](#cite_note-map-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)

#### Android

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Android")]

Main article: [Android (operating system)](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)")

Android is a [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux")-based [operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system") that Google offers as [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") under the [Apache license](/wiki/Apache_license "Apache license"). It is designed primarily for mobile devices such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and tablet computers. Android supports low-cost [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture") systems and others. The first tablets running Android were released in 2009.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Vendors such as [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola")[[136]](#cite_note-136) and [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo")[[137]](#cite_note-137) delayed deployment of their tablets until after 2011, when Android was reworked to include more tablet features.[[138]](#cite_note-138)[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) [Android 3.0 (Honeycomb)](/wiki/Android_Honeycomb "Android Honeycomb"), released in 2011 and later versions support larger screen sizes, mainly tablets, and have access to the [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") service. Android includes operating system, [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware") and key applications. Other vendors sell customized Android tablets, such as [Kindle Fire](/wiki/Kindle_Fire "Kindle Fire") and [Nook](/wiki/Nook "Nook"), which are used to consume [mobile content](/wiki/Mobile_content "Mobile content") and provide their own app store, rather than using the larger [Google Play](/wiki/Google_Play "Google Play") system, thereby fragmenting the Android market.[[141]](#cite_note-141) In 2022 Google began to re-emphasize in-house Android tablet development — at this point, a multi-year commitment.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

##### Android Go

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Android Go")]

Main article: [Comparison of Android Go products § Tablet computers](/wiki/Comparison_of_Android_Go_products#Tablet_computers "Comparison of Android Go products")

A few tablet computers are shipped with [Android Go](/wiki/Android_Go "Android Go").

##### Fire OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Fire OS")]

As mentioned above, [Amazon Fire OS](/wiki/Fire_OS "Fire OS") is an Android-based mobile operating system produced by Amazon for its Fire range of tablets. It is forked from Android. Fire OS primarily centers on content consumption, with a customized user interface and heavy ties to content available from Amazon's own storefronts and services.

#### ChromeOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: ChromeOS")]

Several devices that run [ChromeOS](/wiki/ChromeOS "ChromeOS") came on the market in 2017–2019, as tablets, or as [2-in-1s](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC") with [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") and 360-degree hinge.[[143]](#cite_note-asusChromeOS-143)

#### HarmonyOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: HarmonyOS")]

HarmonyOS (HMOS) ([Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 鸿蒙; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Hóngméng*) is a [distributed operating system](/wiki/Distributed_operating_system "Distributed operating system") developed by [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei") to collaborate and interconnect with multiple smart devices on the [Internet of Things](/wiki/Internet_of_Things "Internet of Things") (IoT) ecosystem.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145) In its current [multi-kernel](/wiki/Multikernel "Multikernel") design, the operating system selects suitable kernels from the [abstraction layer](/wiki/Abstraction_layer "Abstraction layer") for devices with diverse resources.[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147) For IoT devices, the system is known to be based on [LiteOS](/wiki/LiteOS "LiteOS") kernel; while for [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and tablets, it is based on a [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel") layer with [AOSP](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") libraries to support [Android application package](/wiki/Android_application_package "Android application package") (APK) apps using [Android Runtime](/wiki/Android_Runtime "Android Runtime") (ART) through the Ark Compiler, in addition to [native HarmonyOS apps](/wiki/App_(file_format) "App (file format)") built via [integrated development environment](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment") (IDE) known as [DevEco Studio](/wiki/DevEco_Studio "DevEco Studio").[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149)

#### iPadOS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: iPadOS")]

Main articles: [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad"), [iPadOS](/wiki/IPadOS "IPadOS"), and [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS")

The [iPad](/wiki/IPad "IPad") runs on [iPadOS](/wiki/IPadOS "IPadOS"). Prior to the introduction of iPadOS in 2019, the iPad ran [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS"), which was created for the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") and [iPod Touch](/wiki/IPod_Touch "IPod Touch"). The first iPad was released in 2010.[[150]](#cite_note-iPadAvailable-150) Although built on the same underlying [Unix](/wiki/Unix "Unix") implementation as [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), its user interface is radically different. iPadOS is designed for touch input from the user's fingers and has none of the features that required a stylus on earlier tablets. Apple introduced [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch") gestures, such as moving two fingers apart or together to zoom in or out, also termed *pinch to zoom*.[[151]](#cite_note-151) iPadOS and iOS are built for the [ARM architecture](/wiki/ARM_architecture "ARM architecture").[[152]](#cite_note-152)

#### Kindle firmware

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Kindle firmware")]

Main article: [Amazon Kindle](/wiki/Amazon_Kindle "Amazon Kindle")

Kindle firmware is a mobile operating system specifically designed for Amazon Kindle e-readers. It is based on a custom Linux kernel; however, it is entirely closed-source and proprietary, and only runs on Amazon Kindle line up manufactured under the Amazon brand.

#### Nintendo Switch system software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Nintendo Switch system software")]

The [Nintendo Switch system software](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_system_software "Nintendo Switch system software") (also known by its codename Horizon) is an updatable firmware and operating system used by the [Nintendo Switch hybrid video game console/tablet](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") and [Nintendo Switch Lite handheld game console](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch_Lite "Nintendo Switch Lite"). It is based on a proprietary microkernel. The UI includes a HOME screen, consisting of the top bar, the screenshot viewer ("Album"), and shortcuts to the Nintendo eShop, News, and Settings.

#### PlayStation Vita system software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: PlayStation Vita system software")]

The [PlayStation Vita system software](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita_system_software "PlayStation Vita system software") is the official firmware and operating system for the [PlayStation Vita](/wiki/PlayStation_Vita "PlayStation Vita") and [PlayStation TV](/wiki/PlayStation_TV "PlayStation TV") video game consoles. It uses the LiveArea as its graphical shell. The PlayStation Vita system software has one optional add-on component, the PlayStation Mobile Runtime Package. The system is built on a Unix-base which is derived from FreeBSD and NetBSD. Due to it capabilities on browsing the internet and multimedia capabilities, it is treat as an gaming tablet or tablet replacement by community and reviewer/publisher.

#### Ubuntu Touch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ubuntu Touch")]

Main article: [Ubuntu Touch](/wiki/Ubuntu_Touch "Ubuntu Touch")

Ubuntu Touch is an open-source ([GPL](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License")) mobile version of the [Ubuntu](/wiki/Ubuntu "Ubuntu") operating system[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145) originally developed in 2013 by [Canonical Ltd.](/wiki/Canonical_Ltd. "Canonical Ltd.") and continued by the non-profit [UBports Foundation](/w/index.php?title=UBports_Foundation&action=edit&redlink=1 "UBports Foundation (page does not exist)") in 2017.[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) Ubuntu Touch can run on a pure GNU/Linux base on phones with the required drivers, such as the [Librem 5](/wiki/Librem_5 "Librem 5")[[155]](#cite_note-Fossbytes_UbuntuTouch-155) and the [PinePhone](/wiki/PinePhone "PinePhone").[[156]](#cite_note-156) To enable hardware that was originally shipped with Android, Ubuntu Touch makes use of the Android Linux kernel, using Android drivers and services via an [LXC](/wiki/LXC "LXC") container, but does not use any of the Java-like code of Android.[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158) As of February 2022, Ubuntu Touch is available on 78 phones and tablets.[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145)[[159]](#cite_note-159) The UBports Installer serves as an easy-to-use tool to allow inexperienced users to install the operating system on third-party devices without damaging their hardware.[[145]](#cite_note-:0-145)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

#### Windows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Windows")]

Main article: [Windows 10](/wiki/Windows_10 "Windows 10")

Following [Windows for Pen Computing](/wiki/Windows_for_Pen_Computing "Windows for Pen Computing") for [Windows 3.1](/wiki/Windows_3.1x "Windows 3.1x") in 1991, Microsoft supported tablets running Windows XP under the [Microsoft Tablet PC](/wiki/Microsoft_Tablet_PC "Microsoft Tablet PC") name.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Microsoft Tablet PCs were pen-based, fully functional [x86](/wiki/X86 "X86") [PCs](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") with handwriting and voice recognition functionality.[[162]](#cite_note-162) [Windows XP Tablet PC Edition](/wiki/Windows_XP_Tablet_PC_Edition "Windows XP Tablet PC Edition") provided pen support. Tablet support was added to both Home and Business versions of [Windows Vista](/wiki/Windows_Vista "Windows Vista") and [Windows 7](/wiki/Windows_7 "Windows 7"). Tablets running Windows could use the touchscreen for mouse input, hand writing recognition and gesture support. Following Tablet PC, Microsoft announced the [Ultra-mobile PC](/wiki/Ultra-mobile_PC "Ultra-mobile PC") initiative in 2006 which brought Windows tablets to a smaller, touch-centric form factor.[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164) In 2008, Microsoft showed a prototype of a two-screen tablet called [Microsoft Courier](/wiki/Microsoft_Courier "Microsoft Courier"), but cancelled the project.

In 2012, Microsoft released [Windows 8](/wiki/Windows_8 "Windows 8"), which features significant changes to various aspects of the operating system's user interface and platform which are designed for touch-based devices such as tablets. The operating system also introduced an [application store](/wiki/Microsoft_Store "Microsoft Store") and a new style of application optimized primarily for use on tablets.[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-pcw-building-166) Microsoft also introduced [Windows RT](/wiki/Windows_RT "Windows RT"), an edition of Windows 8 for use on ARM-based devices.[[167]](#cite_note-wsj-rtname-167) The launch of Windows 8 and RT was accompanied by the release of devices with the two operating systems by various manufacturers (including Microsoft themselves, with the release of [Surface](/wiki/Microsoft_Surface "Microsoft Surface")), such as slate tablets, hybrids, and convertibles.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

Released in July 2015, Windows 10 introduces what Microsoft described as "[universal apps](/wiki/Universal_app "Universal app")"; expanding on [Metro-style apps](/wiki/Metro-style_app "Metro-style app"), these apps can be designed to run across multiple Microsoft product families with nearly identical code‍ – ‌including [PCs](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer"), tablets, [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), [embedded systems](/wiki/Embedded_system "Embedded system"), [Xbox One](/wiki/Xbox_One "Xbox One"), [Surface Hub](/wiki/Surface_Hub "Surface Hub") and [Windows Holographic](/wiki/Windows_Holographic "Windows Holographic"). The Windows user interface was revised to handle transitions between a mouse-oriented interface and a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen")-optimized interface based on available input devices‍ – ‌particularly on [2-in-1 PCs](/wiki/2-in-1_PC "2-in-1 PC"); both interfaces include an updated [Start menu](/wiki/Start_menu "Start menu"). Windows 10 replaced all earlier editions of Windows.[[169]](#cite_note-169)[[170]](#cite_note-170)

#### Hybrid OS operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Hybrid OS operation")]

Several hardware companies have built hybrid devices with the possibility to work with both Android and [Windows Phone](/wiki/Windows_Phone "Windows Phone") operating systems (or in rare cases [Windows 8.1](/wiki/Windows_8.1 "Windows 8.1"), as with the, by now cancelled, [Asus Transformer Book Duet](/wiki/Asus_Transformer_Book_Duet "Asus Transformer Book Duet")), while [Ars Technica](/wiki/Ars_Technica "Ars Technica") stated: "dual-OS devices are always terrible products. Windows and Android almost never cross-communicate, so any dual-OS device means dealing with separate apps, data, and storage pools and completely different UI paradigms. So from a consumer perspective, Microsoft and Google are really just saving OEMs from producing tons of clunky devices that no one will want."[[171]](#cite_note-171)

### Discontinued tablet operating systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Discontinued tablet operating systems")]

#### BlackBerry 10

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: BlackBerry 10")]

[BlackBerry 10](/wiki/BlackBerry_10 "BlackBerry 10") (based on the [QNX](/wiki/QNX "QNX") OS) is from [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited"). As a smartphone OS, it is closed-source and proprietary, and only runs on phones and tablets manufactured by BlackBerry.

One of the dominant platforms in the world in the late 2000s, its global market share was reduced significantly by the mid-2010s. In late 2016, BlackBerry announced that it will continue to support the OS, with a promise to release 10.3.3.[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-IDC.com-173) Therefore, BlackBerry 10 would not receive any major updates as BlackBerry and its partners would focus more on their Android base development.[[174]](#cite_note-174)

#### BlackBerry Tablet OS

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: BlackBerry Tablet OS")]

[BlackBerry Tablet OS](/wiki/BlackBerry_Tablet_OS "BlackBerry Tablet OS") is an operating system from [BlackBerry Ltd](/wiki/BlackBerry_Ltd "BlackBerry Ltd") based on the [QNX Neutrino](/wiki/QNX_Neutrino "QNX Neutrino") [real-time operating system](/wiki/Real-time_operating_system "Real-time operating system") designed to run [Adobe AIR](/wiki/Adobe_AIR "Adobe AIR") and [BlackBerry WebWorks](/wiki/BlackBerry_WebWorks "BlackBerry WebWorks") applications, currently available for the [BlackBerry PlayBook](/wiki/BlackBerry_PlayBook "BlackBerry PlayBook") tablet computer.
The BlackBerry Tablet OS is the first tablet running an operating system from [QNX](/wiki/QNX "QNX") (now a subsidiary of RIM[[175]](#cite_note-175)).

BlackBerry Tablet OS supports standard BlackBerry [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") applications. Support for [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") apps has also been announced, through sandbox "app players" which can be ported by developers or installed through sideloading by users.[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177) A BlackBerry Tablet OS Native Development Kit, to develop native applications with the [GNU toolchain](/wiki/GNU_toolchain "GNU toolchain") is currently in closed beta testing. The first device to run BlackBerry Tablet OS was the [BlackBerry PlayBook](/wiki/BlackBerry_PlayBook "BlackBerry PlayBook") tablet computer.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

### Application store

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Application store")]

See also: [App store](/wiki/App_store "App store")

[Apps](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") that do not come pre-installed with the system are supplied through [online distribution](/wiki/List_of_mobile_app_distribution_platforms "List of mobile app distribution platforms"). These sources, termed *[app stores](/wiki/App_store "App store")*, provide centralized catalogs of software and allow "one click" on-device software purchasing, installation and updates.[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)

Mobile device suppliers may adopt a ["walled garden"](/wiki/Walled_garden_(technology) "Walled garden (technology)") approach, wherein the supplier controls what software applications ("apps") are available. [Software development kits](/wiki/Software_development_kit "Software development kit") are restricted to approved software developers. This can be used to reduce the impact of [malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware"), provide software with an approved [content rating](/wiki/Content_rating "Content rating"), control application quality and exclude competing vendors.[[181]](#cite_note-181) [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Amazon](/wiki/Amazon_(company) "Amazon (company)"), Microsoft and [Barnes & Noble](/wiki/Barnes_%26_Noble "Barnes & Noble") all adopted the strategy. B&N originally allowed arbitrary apps to be installed,[[182]](#cite_note-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) but, in December 2011, excluded third parties.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187)[[188]](#cite_note-188) Apple and IBM have agreed to cooperate in [cross-selling](/wiki/Cross-selling "Cross-selling") IBM-developed applications for iPads and iPhones in enterprise-level accounts.[[189]](#cite_note-189) Proponents of open source software say that the iPad (or such "walled garden" app store approach) violates the spirit of personal control that traditional personal computers have always provided.[[190]](#cite_note-iPad_is_iBad_for_freedom-190)[[191]](#cite_note-The_iPad_Is_Not_a_Computer-191)[[192]](#cite_note-popsci.com-192)

## Sales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tablet_computer&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Sales")]

Around 2010, tablet use by businesses jumped, as business began to use them for conferences, events, and trade shows.
